text: Overtraining syndrome (OTS) requires both suppressed performance for more than 4 weeks and accompanying changes in psychological symptoms for diagnosis.
No published studies have provided objective evidence tracking performance changes from a healthy baseline through to a diagnosed overtrained state with prolonged performance suppression.
Vague and inconsistent terminology around overtraining, overreaching, and staleness hampers diagnosis and research into overtraining syndrome.
No ideal, easily repeatable testing battery currently exists for regularly monitoring athletes for overtraining syndrome onset.
Prospective longitudinal monitoring in real-world training settings is needed to understand how physiological and psychological symptoms of overtraining manifest over time.
Overtraining syndrome diagnosis requires tracking both physiological performance markers and psychological mood state changes, as neither alone is sufficient.
